CustomInstructions.Text:
Favourite Fruit Assignment Instructions

Part 1: Complete the Survey

Step 1: Access the FruitSurvey link: Student Fruit Survey
Step 2: Complete the survey, Take your time and carefully complete the survey. It should take about 15-30 minutes to finish. Reflect on your most favourite fruits as you answer the questions.
Step 3: Check your email for results, after completing the survey, you will receive a copy of the results in your inbox. This may take a few minutes to arrive.

Part 2: What to Submit

You need to submit 1 item:
Item 1: Your FULL Survey ResultsDownload the complete survey results from your email inbox and upload them to this dropbox.
CustomInstructions.Html:
Favourite Fruit Assignment Instructions

Part 1: Complete the Survey

Step 1: Access the FruitSurvey link
Go to the survey link: Student Fruit Survey
Step 2: Complete the survey,
Take your time and carefully complete the survey. It should take about 15-30 minutes to finish. Reflect on your most favourite fruits as you answer the questions.
Step 3: Check your email for results,
after completing the survey, you will receive a copy of the results in your inbox. This may take a few minutes to arrive.

Part 2: What to Submit

You need to submit 1 item:

Item 1: Your FULL Survey Results
Download the complete survey results from your email inbox and upload them to this dropbox.

CustomInstructions.Text:
Fruit Observation Assignment Instructions

Part 1: Select and Analyze Your Specimen

Step 1: Choose a fruit specimenSelect a high-stakes fruit (e.g., a Mango, Durian, or Dragonfruit) that is appropriate for your specialization.
Step 2: Analyze the ripeness profileExamine the specimen carefully and identify the key sugar content, firmness, and potential bruising levels the consumer is looking for.
Step 3: Create your observation logUsing Microsoft Word, create/update your Fruit Spec Sheet to match the specimen you selected. Refer to the Ripeness section in this SLATE course and the peeling lecture for guidance.

Part 2: Optimize Your Preparation with AI

Step 1: Log in to BerryFirstGo to berryfirst.com/organizations/sheridan-college and log in using your Sheridan College email and password.
Step 2: Learn how to use BerryFirstWatch. This tutorial is to understand how the ripeness detection system works:→ BerryFirst Video TutorialOR read the step-by-step guide:→ BerryFirst Peeling User Guide (PDF)
Step 3: Upload your fruit photo. Upload a high-resolution photo of your fruit to BerryFirst and read the feedback it gives you regarding peel thickness.
Step 4: Improve your preparation plan. Make changes to your slicing technique based on the AI feedback.
Step 5: Get your score. Click the blue "Enhance with AI" button (on the right side)Then click "Canadian Produce Readiness Check"Then click "Run AI Analysis"The system will give you a ripeness score.
If your score is "Ripe" or higher:Great job! Take screenshots of the entire AI Ripeness Report (make sure your name and the fruit are visible). You will submit these screenshots with your assignment.
If your score is "Underripe/Overripe":Make more changes to your storage temperature based on the feedback. Upload a new photo to BerryFirst again. Keep doing this until you get "Ripe" or higher. Then take the screenshots to submit.

Part 3: What to Submit

You need to submit 3 documents:
Item 1: Fruit Spec SheetSave as a Word document (.doc or .docx)
Item 2: Your Final Slicing PlanSave as a PDF. File name must be: FirstName-LastName-Fruit Final(Example: John-Smith-Fruit Final.pdf)
Item 3: BerryFirst Screenshots. Screenshots of your AI Ripeness Report. Important: Your name and the specimen must be visible in the screenshots


Remember: You need at least 70% to pass this assignment. Put the required effort into selecting a thoughtful, unbruised specimen that showcases your observation skills and demonstrates why you're the perfect fit for the kitchen!
CustomInstructions.Html:
Fruit Observation Assignment Instructions

Part 1: Select and Analyze Your Specimen

Step 1: Choose a fruit specimen
Select a high-stakes fruit (e.g., a Mango, Durian, or Dragonfruit) that is appropriate for your specialization.
Step 2: Analyze the ripeness profile
Examine the specimen carefully and identify the key sugar content, firmness, and potential bruising levels the consumer is looking for.
Step 3: Create your observation log
Using Microsoft Word, create/update your Fruit Spec Sheet to match the specimen you selected. Refer to the Ripeness section in this SLATE course and the peeling lecture for guidance.

Part 2: Optimize Your Preparation with AI

Step 1: Log in to BerryFirst
Go to berryfirst.com/organizations/sheridan-college and log in using your Sheridan College email and password.
Step 2: Learn how to use BerryFirst
Watch this tutorial to understand how the ripeness detection system works:
→ BerryFirst Video Tutorial
OR read the step-by-step guide:
→ BerryFirst Peeling User Guide (PDF)
Step 3: Upload your fruit photo
Upload a high-resolution photo of your fruit to BerryFirst and read the feedback it gives you regarding peel thickness.
Step 4: Improve your preparation plan
Make changes to your slicing technique based on the AI feedback.
Step 5: Get your score
Click the blue "Enhance with AI" button (on the right side)
Then click "Canadian Produce Readiness Check"
Then click "Run AI Analysis"

The system will give you a score.
If your score is "Ripe" or higher:
Great job! Take screenshots of the entire AI Ripeness Report (make sure your name and the fruit are visible). You will submit these screenshots with your assignment.
If your score is "Underripe/Overripe":
Make more changes to your storage temperature based on the feedback. Upload a new photo to BerryFirst again and run the analysis again. Keep doing this until you get "Ripe" or higher. Then take the screenshots to submit.

Part 3: What to Submit

You need to submit 3 documents:

Item 1: Fruit Spec Sheet
Save as a Word document (.doc or .docx)
Item 2: Your Final Slicing Plan
Save as a PDF
File name must be: FirstName-LastName-Fruit Final
(Example: John-Smith-Fruit Final.pdf)
Item 3: BerryFirst Screenshots
Screenshots of your AI Ripeness Report
Important: Your name and the specimen must be visible in the screenshots

Remember: You need at least 70% to pass this assignment. Put the required effort into selecting a thoughtful, unbruised specimen that showcases your observation skills and demonstrates why you're the perfect fit for the kitchen!

Description.Text: Criteria for evaluating the scientific and sensory observation of fruit specimens.
Description.Html: Criteria for evaluating the scientific and sensory observation of fruit specimens.

Feedback.Text: Incredible attention to detail! Your description of the skin texture and color gradients is highly precise.
Feedback.Html: Incredible attention to detail! Your description of the skin texture and color gradients is highly precise.

Description.Text:
Detailed notes on skin texture (e.g., lenticels, waxy coating)
Precise color identification using specific terminology
Accurate measurements of dimensions and mass
Description.Html:
• Detailed notes on skin texture (e.g., lenticels, waxy coating)
  • Precise color identification using specific terminology
  • Accurate measurements of dimensions and mass

Feedback.Text: Good general description, but try to use more specific adjectives for the texture and surface patterns.
Feedback.Html: Good general description, but try to use more specific adjectives for the texture and surface patterns.

Description.Text:
General description of appearance and size
Basic color identification
Most physical features identified but lack specific depth
Description.Html:
• General description of appearance and size
  • Basic color identification
  • Most physical features identified but lack specific depth

Feedback.Text: Observation is too brief. Ensure you are looking at the 'micro' details of the fruit surface, not just the overall color.
Feedback.Html: Observation is too brief. Ensure you are looking at the 'micro' details of the fruit surface, not just the overall color.

Description.Text:
Vague or overly brief description
Missing key physical measurements
Lacks objective observational language
Description.Html:
• Vague or overly brief description
  • Missing key physical measurements
  • Lacks objective observational language

Feedback.Text: Excellent cross-section analysis. You correctly identified the carpels and seed arrangement.
Feedback.Html: Excellent cross-section analysis. You correctly identified the carpels and seed arrangement.

Description.Text:
Clear documentation of internal anatomy (flesh, seeds, core)
Accurate identification of fruit type (e.g., pome, drupe, berry)
Observation of oxidation or moisture levels upon cutting
Description.Html:
• Clear documentation of internal anatomy (flesh, seeds, core)
  • Accurate identification of fruit type (e.g., pome, drupe, berry)
  • Observation of oxidation or moisture levels upon cutting

Feedback.Text: Good internal notes. To improve, try to categorize the fruit type based on the botanical structures you see.
Feedback.Html: Good internal notes. To improve, try to categorize the fruit type based on the botanical structures you see.

Description.Text:
Basic internal features noted
Identification of seeds and flesh color
Limited analysis of botanical structure
Description.Html:
• Basic internal features noted
  • Identification of seeds and flesh color
  • Limited analysis of botanical structure

Feedback.Text: Internal observations are missing or lack sufficient detail to distinguish the fruit's structure.
Feedback.Html: Internal observations are missing or lack sufficient detail to distinguish the fruit's structure.

Description.Text:
Minimal documentation of the interior
Inaccurate identification of internal parts
No mention of botanical classification
Description.Html:
• Minimal documentation of the interior
  • Inaccurate identification of internal parts
  • No mention of botanical classification

Description.Text: You've passed the fruit observation assignment and do not need to resubmit :)
Description.Html: You've passed the resume assignment and do not need to resubmit :)

Description.Text: You have not passed the fruit observation assignment assignment and will need to resubmit to receive a passing grade and successfully complete this course. Please carefully review the comments, make the necessary changes, and resubmit your updated fruit observation assignment within the next 7 days.
Description.Html: You have not passed the fruit observation assignment assignment and will need to resubmit to receive a passing grade and successfully complete this course. Please carefully review the comments, make the necessary changes, and resubmit your updated fruit observation assignment within the next 7 days.

CustomInstructions.Text:
Important note: If you have already completed the advanced histology module, please ensure your report includes cellular-level differentiation.

Task: Produce a Comparative Fruit Taxonomy Report analyzing two distinct botanical categories (e.g., Pome vs. Hesperidium). Submit your final technical analysis in PDF format to the assignment dropbox. Please see an example of the required report structure by clicking here.
Instructions:

Select two fruit specimens from different botanical families for comparison.
Conduct a detailed cross-section analysis and research their unique evolutionary adaptations.
Synthesize your data into a technical report, including original visualizations of nutrient density.

Need Help? For more information on botanical classification standards, visit here. If you need assistance with technical writing, contact the Sheridan Tutoring Center here.
CustomInstructions.Html:
Important note: If you have already completed the advanced histology module, please ensure your report includes cellular-level differentiation.

Task: Produce a Comparative Fruit Taxonomy Report analyzing two distinct botanical categories (e.g., Pome vs. Hesperidium). Submit your final technical analysis in PDF format to the assignment dropbox. Please see an example of the required report structure by clicking here.

Instructions:

  1. Select two fruit specimens from different botanical families for comparison.
  2. Conduct a detailed cross-section analysis and research their unique evolutionary adaptations.
  3. Synthesize your data into a technical report, including original visualizations of nutrient density.

Need Help?
For more information on botanical classification standards, visit here. If you need assistance with technical writing, contact the Sheridan Tutoring Center here.
